Q1: My study routine was honestly quite scattered because I'm juggling a lot at 28 with limited resources. I would typically try to carve out time in the evenings after work, maybe two or three hours when I could focus. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd often listen to lecture recordings while doing other tasks or commuting. Some weeks I managed to get in good study blocks, other weeks life just got in the way and I had to make do with whatever time I could grab.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was happening in my life and when deadlines were approaching. I couldn't maintain a strict schedule because of work and other responsibilities that come with being an older student. When exams or big assignments were coming up, I'd push harder and find more time somehow. But during quieter periods, I might go days without touching the course materials because other priorities took over.
Q3: Usually a deadline or an upcoming exam would prompt me to log in. Sometimes I'd get a notification about new content or a discussion post and that would remind me to check in. To be honest, I wasn't logging in just to browse around—I went in with purpose when I needed specific materials or had to submit something. The LMS wasn't something I checked habitually like social media.
Q4: I can't say it ever truly became routine for me. There were brief stretches where I'd log in more regularly, maybe when a particular module really captured my interest. But with only logging in about three times a week and my low engagement overall, it never became automatic. I think if I had more consistent time and fewer outside pressures, it might have become more habitual.
Q5: The audio lectures and recorded discussions were gold for me because I learn best by listening. I could download them and listen during my commute or while doing chores at home. The discussion forums were also helpful when classmates explained concepts in different ways—hearing multiple perspectives helped things click. Video content was useful too, though I couldn't always watch the full 46 minutes in one sitting.
Q6: I often skipped the reading materials and supplementary PDFs because I struggle to stay focused with text-heavy content. My auditory learning style means I retain more from listening than reading. I also minimally used the interactive quizzes because they felt time-consuming and I was always short on time. If something wasn't directly tied to an assignment or exam, it usually got pushed aside.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly helped me understand something I was struggling with or if it counted toward my grade. When I could see immediate relevance to assignments or exams, I was motivated to engage. I also valued activities that I could do flexibly—like listening to recordings—because my schedule is unpredictable. Anything that felt like busywork without clear benefit got deprioritized quickly.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—they were the first things to go when time got tight. Required activities had a sense of urgency attached to them that optional ones just didn't have. I always intended to do the optional stuff, but when you're stretched thin, you focus on what's mandatory. It's not that I didn't see value in them; I just couldn't justify the time investment when assignments were waiting.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline and away from the LMS. I'd download audio files and PDFs to my phone and study during my commute or breaks at work. I also used free YouTube videos and podcasts to supplement what I was learning in the course. Sometimes I'd write notes by hand from memory after listening to lectures, which helped me process the information better.
Q10: There were many times I was deeply engaged with course material but the LMS wouldn't show it. I'd spend an hour listening to a downloaded lecture while walking, then another hour reviewing my handwritten notes at home. I also discussed concepts with a friend who's in a similar field—we'd talk through problems together. None of that shows up in any system, but it was real learning happening.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my main approach. I'd access a video or audio file once, download it, and then engage with it multiple times offline. My internet isn't always reliable and data costs money, so downloading was more practical. I'd often listen to the same lecture two or three times while doing different activities throughout the week. The LMS would only show that single access, not my repeated engagement.
Q12: There were definitely harder periods, especially when work got demanding or when I felt overwhelmed by how much content there was. Sometimes the sheer volume of 14 courses felt crushing, and I'd feel paralyzed about where to start. Financial stress also crept in occasionally, making it hard to focus on academics. During those times, I'd do the bare minimum to stay afloat rather than truly engaging.
Q13: Fatigue was probably my biggest enemy—after a long day of work, sitting down to study required real willpower. Competing responsibilities definitely affected my LMS usage because I had to prioritize what was most urgent. When I was confused about course content, I'd sometimes avoid the LMS because I didn't know where to begin. The workload across all my courses meant I couldn't give each one the attention it deserved.
Q14: There were times I'd open videos or materials but my mind was elsewhere entirely. I'd be too tired to actually absorb anything, just going through the motions. Sometimes I'd have the lecture playing but I'd be thinking about bills or work issues. Those sessions felt wasted even though technically I was 'accessing' the course materials.
Q15: I definitely postponed discussion posts and some assignments even when I knew they mattered. Part of it was feeling like I needed to understand the material better before contributing, which became a cycle of delay. Sometimes the anxiety of not doing well made me avoid starting altogether. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I was fresher, but tomorrow brought its own challenges.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my primary motivator for LMS activity. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find time I didn't know I had. My engagement would spike dramatically in the days before something was due, then drop off after. It's not the healthiest pattern, but with everything else going on, deadlines created the urgency I needed to prioritize coursework.
Q17: When I got good feedback on assignments, it motivated me to engage more with similar activities. My assignment scores were actually quite high, which surprised me and gave me confidence. But feedback often came too late to really change my approach for the next task. Positive feedback definitely lifted my mood and made me feel like my effort was worth it despite my circumstances.
Q18: The course structure was somewhat flexible, which I appreciated given my irregular schedule. However, having 14 online courses meant the structure of each one kind of blurred together. When modules were clearly organized with audio options, I engaged more. But when courses were heavily text-based or required synchronous participation, I struggled to keep up.
Q19: Having downloadable content made engagement much easier for me given my learning style and limited resources. Clear deadlines and organized modules helped me plan my limited study time. What made it harder was when important information was buried in long text documents or when discussions required real-time participation. The mindfulness intervention actually helped me manage stress around engagement.
Q20: I think those students might be like me—searching for what they need, downloading things, trying to understand the system. High activity could mean someone is struggling to find the right resources or is anxious about missing something. It could also be technical issues causing multiple accesses. Activity doesn't equal productivity; sometimes you're just trying to orient yourself.
Q21: This definitely reflects my experience to some degree. My exam score was decent and my assignment scores were actually very high, but my LMS activity was low. I did a lot of learning offline through downloaded materials and personal study methods. Some people are efficient—they know what they need, get it, and work independently. The LMS doesn't capture that kind of focused, offline engagement.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show login patterns, submission times, and which resources were accessed. They're good at tracking deadlines and identifying students who might be completely disengaged. But they miss the quality of engagement, offline studying, and the mental effort someone puts in. They can't tell if someone listened to a lecture five times on their phone or discussed concepts with peers outside the platform.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious about a topic and find myself thinking about it even outside study time. For me, engagement is also about connecting new knowledge to my life experience and goals. It's not just about being present in the LMS—it's about being mentally invested in learning.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I've grown personally through this course despite my challenges. It can't capture the conversations I have with family about what I'm learning or how I apply concepts at work. My resilience in continuing despite limited resources and competing demands isn't visible in any log. The fact that I'm surprised by my own success and pushing through at 28—that human story gets lost in the data.
